explanation_md: `MyErr` derives from `std::runtime_error`, so the first catch (`const std::runtime_error&`) matches it. The second catch is never reached. This is the ordering mistake: derived types must come before their base in catch sequences.

prompt_md: You derive `NetworkError` from `std::runtime_error` and want to catch it specifically. Which catch order is correct?

options.c: Order doesn't matter; C++ resolves the most-derived match automatically
options.d: You cannot catch both in the same try block

explanation_md: Catch clauses are checked in written order. `std::runtime_error` is a base of `NetworkError`, so it would match `NetworkError` objects if listed first. Put the most-derived catch first so it gets the chance to run.
`AppError` is caught by its own specific handler. `e.code` is `42` and `e.what()` returns `"fail"` because the message was correctly forwarded to the `std::runtime_error` constructor.

options.b: The base constructor is called with `""` instead of `msg`, so the message is lost
options.d: Custom exception classes cannot inherit `what()` from `std::runtime_error`

explanation_md: `std::runtime_error` stores the message passed to its constructor and returns it via `what()`. Here the base constructor is called with `""` instead of `msg`, discarding the actual message `"boom"`. Fix: `: std::runtime_error(msg)`.

prompt_md: Why should you always catch exceptions by `const` reference rather than by value, especially when working with derived exception classes?

options.a: The compiler requires const reference for all exception types
options.b: Catching by value slices the object, losing the derived class data
options.c: Catching by value copies the exception twice, causing double-free errors
options.d: References are faster at runtime due to cache locality

explanation_md: If you catch `std::runtime_error e` by value and the thrown type is `DatabaseError` (derived from `std::runtime_error`), only the `std::runtime_error` base part is copied into `e`. The derived data (like `error_code`) is sliced away. Catching by `const` reference avoids this: `e` refers to the actual thrown object without copying.
